entries.title: Urban Legend: The Hook
entries.hook: Think you know this story? Think again:
entries.myth: A couple parked at Lover's Lane hears a radio warning about an escaped convict with a hook for a hand. Later they find a bloody hook dangling from the car door handle.
entries.truth: The story first appeared in 1950s teen folklore magazines. No real incident has ever matched the details.

entries.title: Urban Legend: Room for One More
entries.hook: You won't believe this story is fake:
entries.myth: A taxi picks up a passenger who gets out at a cemetery. The driver looks back and the passenger has vanished. The person died exactly one year ago.
entries.truth: This is a global folklore motif dating back centuries with versions in Japanese Mexican and European cultures.

entries.title: Urban Legend: The Clown Statue
entries.hook: Everyone knows this urban legend. Almost none of it is true:
entries.myth: A family buys a creepy clown statue that keeps appearing in different rooms. They learn it was never a statue but a man playing dead.
entries.truth: This story gained traction on early internet forums in the 2000s. It is a variation of the living statue trope found in horror fiction dating to the 1800s.

entries.title: Urban Legend: The Crying Boy
entries.hook: You've heard this story. But here's what really happened:
entries.myth: A painting of a crying boy is blamed for causing house fires. The painting is always found intact on the wall.
entries.truth: A 1985 UK newspaper claimed firefighters found the print untouched in fires. The real reason: it was mass-produced so appeared in many homes. Confirmation bias created the legend.

entries.title: Urban Legend: Alligators in the Sewers
entries.hook: You won't believe this story is fake:
entries.myth: NYC's sewers are infested with alligators that were flushed as babies and grew feeding on rats in the dark tunnels.
entries.truth: The myth started in the 1930s when a few small alligators were found in sewers. They were dumped by owners not a breeding population. Sewers are too cold for them.

entries.title: Urban Legend: Bloody Mary
entries.hook: The truth behind this myth is shocking:
entries.myth: Say Bloody Mary three times in front of a mirror and a ghostly woman appears to attack you. The legend has terrified children at sleepovers for decades.
entries.truth: The legend likely originated from 16th century Queen Mary I. The modern version spread in the 1970s as a harmless dare game inspired by mirror-gazing superstitions.

entries.title: Urban Legend: Spider Bite
entries.hook: You probably believe this urban legend. Here's the truth:
entries.myth: A woman bitten by a spider goes to the doctor who finds hundreds of baby spiders crawling out of the wound.
entries.truth: Medically impossible. Spider eggs cannot survive in human tissue. The story originated from a 1990s chain email.

entries.title: Urban Legend: The Kidney Heist
entries.hook: Everyone knows this urban legend. Almost none of it is true:
entries.myth: A businessman wakes up in a bathtub of ice with a note saying 'Call 911 you've had a kidney removed.'
entries.truth: No verified case exists. Kidney transplants require tissue matching and medical infrastructure. The story spread via late-90s chain emails.

entries.title: Urban Legend: The Crying Boy
entries.hook: You probably believe this urban legend. Here's the truth:
entries.myth: A painting of a crying boy is blamed for causing house fires. The painting is always found intact on the wall.
entries.truth: A 1985 UK newspaper claimed firefighters found the print untouched in fires. The real reason: it was mass-produced so appeared in many homes. Confirmation bias created the legend.

entries.title: Urban Legend: Spider Bite

entries.myth: A woman bitten by a spider goes to the doctor who finds hundreds of baby spiders crawling out of the wound.

entries.title: Urban Legend: Room for One More
entries.hook: Everyone knows this urban legend. Almost none of it is true:
entries.myth: A taxi picks up a passenger who gets out at a cemetery. The driver looks back and the passenger has vanished. The person died exactly one year ago.
entries.truth: This is a global folklore motif dating back centuries with versions in Japanese Mexican and European cultures.

entries.title: Urban Legend: The Crying Boy
entries.hook: Here's what really happened:
entries.myth: A painting of a crying boy is blamed for causing house fires. The painting is always found intact on the wall.
entries.truth: A 1985 UK newspaper claimed firefighters found the print untouched in fires. The real reason: it was mass-produced so appeared in many homes. Confirmation bias created the legend.

entries.title: Urban Legend: Bloody Mary
entries.hook: This famous story is completely made up. Here's the real origin:
entries.myth: Say Bloody Mary three times in front of a mirror and a ghostly woman appears to attack you. The legend has terrified children at sleepovers for decades.
entries.truth: The legend likely originated from 16th century Queen Mary I. The modern version spread in the 1970s as a harmless dare game inspired by mirror-gazing superstitions.

entries.title: Urban Legend: Alligators in the Sewers
entries.hook: Think you know this story? Think again:
entries.myth: NYC's sewers are infested with alligators that were flushed as babies and grew feeding on rats in the dark tunnels.
entries.truth: The myth started in the 1930s when a few small alligators were found in sewers. They were dumped by owners not a breeding population. Sewers are too cold for them.

entries.title: Urban Legend: Spider Bite

entries.myth: A woman bitten by a spider goes to the doctor who finds hundreds of baby spiders crawling out of the wound.

entries.title: Urban Legend: The Kidney Heist
entries.hook: This famous story is completely made up. Here's the real origin:
entries.myth: A businessman wakes up in a bathtub of ice with a note saying 'Call 911 you've had a kidney removed.'

entries.title: Urban Legend: Alligators in the Sewers
entries.hook: This famous story is completely made up. Here's the real origin:
entries.myth: NYC's sewers are infested with alligators that were flushed as babies and grew feeding on rats in the dark tunnels.
entries.truth: The myth started in the 1930s when a few small alligators were found in sewers. They were dumped by owners not a breeding population. Sewers are too cold for them.

entries.title: Urban Legend: Bloody Mary

entries.myth: Say Bloody Mary three times in front of a mirror and a ghostly woman appears to attack you. The legend has terrified children at sleepovers for decades.

entries.title: Urban Legend: The Crying Boy
entries.hook: You've heard this story. But here's what really happened:
entries.myth: A painting of a crying boy is blamed for causing house fires. The painting is always found intact on the wall.
entries.truth: A 1985 UK newspaper claimed firefighters found the print untouched in fires. The real reason: it was mass-produced so appeared in many homes. Confirmation bias created the legend.

entries.title: Urban Legend: Alligators in the Sewers
entries.hook: The scariest story you've heard? It's not what you think:
entries.myth: NYC's sewers are infested with alligators that were flushed as babies and grew feeding on rats in the dark tunnels.
entries.truth: The myth started in the 1930s when a few small alligators were found in sewers. They were dumped by owners not a breeding population. Sewers are too cold for them.

entries.title: Urban Legend: The Crying Boy

entries.myth: A painting of a crying boy is blamed for causing house fires. The painting is always found intact on the wall.

entries.title: Urban Legend: Room for One More

entries.title: Urban Legend: The Crying Boy
entries.hook: Here's what really happened:
entries.myth: A painting of a crying boy is blamed for causing house fires. The painting is always found intact on the wall.
entries.truth: A 1985 UK newspaper claimed firefighters found the print untouched in fires. The real reason: it was mass-produced so appeared in many homes. Confirmation bias created the legend.

entries.title: Urban Legend: Bloody Mary
entries.hook: Here's what really happened:
entries.myth: Say Bloody Mary three times in front of a mirror and a ghostly woman appears to attack you. The legend has terrified children at sleepovers for decades.
entries.truth: The legend likely originated from 16th century Queen Mary I. The modern version spread in the 1970s as a harmless dare game inspired by mirror-gazing superstitions.

entries.title: Urban Legend: Alligators in the Sewers

entries.myth: NYC's sewers are infested with alligators that were flushed as babies and grew feeding on rats in the dark tunnels.

entries.title: Urban Legend: The Crying Boy
entries.hook: The truth behind this myth is shocking:
entries.myth: A painting of a crying boy is blamed for causing house fires. The painting is always found intact on the wall.
entries.truth: A 1985 UK newspaper claimed firefighters found the print untouched in fires. The real reason: it was mass-produced so appeared in many homes. Confirmation bias created the legend.

entries.title: Urban Legend: The Kidney Heist

entries.myth: A businessman wakes up in a bathtub of ice with a note saying 'Call 911 you've had a kidney removed.'

entries.title: Urban Legend: The Licked Hand
entries.hook: Everyone knows this urban legend. Almost none of it is true:
entries.myth: A girl staying home alone puts her hand down for her dog to lick. In the morning the dog is dead with a message 'Humans can lick too.'
entries.truth: This story appeared in a 1992 horror fiction collection. No police report of this event exists anywhere.

entries.title: Urban Legend: The Crying Boy
entries.hook: The truth behind this myth is shocking:
entries.myth: A painting of a crying boy is blamed for causing house fires. The painting is always found intact on the wall.
entries.truth: A 1985 UK newspaper claimed firefighters found the print untouched in fires. The real reason: it was mass-produced so appeared in many homes. Confirmation bias created the legend.

entries.title: Urban Legend: Bloody Mary
entries.hook: You won't believe this story is fake:
entries.myth: Say Bloody Mary three times in front of a mirror and a ghostly woman appears to attack you. The legend has terrified children at sleepovers for decades.
entries.truth: The legend likely originated from 16th century Queen Mary I. The modern version spread in the 1970s as a harmless dare game inspired by mirror-gazing superstitions.

entries.title: Urban Legend: Spider Bite
entries.hook: Think you know this story? Think again:
entries.myth: A woman bitten by a spider goes to the doctor who finds hundreds of baby spiders crawling out of the wound.
entries.truth: Medically impossible. Spider eggs cannot survive in human tissue. The story originated from a 1990s chain email.

entries.title: Urban Legend: Bloody Mary
entries.hook: You probably believe this urban legend. Here's the truth:
entries.myth: Say Bloody Mary three times in front of a mirror and a ghostly woman appears to attack you. The legend has terrified children at sleepovers for decades.
entries.truth: The legend likely originated from 16th century Queen Mary I. The modern version spread in the 1970s as a harmless dare game inspired by mirror-gazing superstitions.

entries.title: Urban Legend: The Crying Boy
entries.hook: This famous story is completely made up. Here's the real origin:
entries.myth: A painting of a crying boy is blamed for causing house fires. The painting is always found intact on the wall.
entries.truth: A 1985 UK newspaper claimed firefighters found the print untouched in fires. The real reason: it was mass-produced so appeared in many homes. Confirmation bias created the legend.

entries.title: Urban Legend: The Babysitter
entries.hook: Here's what really happened:
entries.myth: A babysitter receives creepy calls asking 'Have you checked the children?' The police say the calls are coming from inside the house where the killer is upstairs.
entries.truth: This story appeared in a 1960s horror anthology. No real case matches the exact scenario but it became one of the most retold urban legends.

entries.title: Urban Legend: Alligators in the Sewers
entries.hook: This famous story is completely made up. Here's the real origin:
entries.myth: NYC's sewers are infested with alligators that were flushed as babies and grew feeding on rats in the dark tunnels.
entries.truth: The myth started in the 1930s when a few small alligators were found in sewers. They were dumped by owners not a breeding population. Sewers are too cold for them.

But here's the truth: The myth started in the 1930s when a few small alligators were found in sewers. They were dumped by owners not a breeding population. Sewers are too cold for them.

entries.title: Urban Legend: Bloody Mary
entries.hook: Here's what really happened:
entries.myth: Say Bloody Mary three times in front of a mirror and a ghostly woman appears to attack you. The legend has terrified children at sleepovers for decades.
entries.truth: The legend likely originated from 16th century Queen Mary I. The modern version spread in the 1970s as a harmless dare game inspired by mirror-gazing superstitions.

entries.title: Urban Legend: The Crying Boy
entries.hook: The truth behind this myth is shocking:
entries.myth: A painting of a crying boy is blamed for causing house fires. The painting is always found intact on the wall.
entries.truth: A 1985 UK newspaper claimed firefighters found the print untouched in fires. The real reason: it was mass-produced so appeared in many homes. Confirmation bias created the legend.

entries.title: Urban Legend: The Hook
entries.hook: Think you know this story? Think again:
entries.myth: A couple parked at Lover's Lane hears a radio warning about an escaped convict with a hook for a hand. Later they find a bloody hook dangling from the car door handle.
entries.truth: The story first appeared in 1950s teen folklore magazines. No real incident has ever matched the details.

entries.title: Urban Legend: The Licked Hand
entries.hook: The scariest story you've heard? It's not what you think:
entries.myth: A girl staying home alone puts her hand down for her dog to lick. In the morning the dog is dead with a message 'Humans can lick too.'

entries.title: Urban Legend: Alligators in the Sewers
entries.hook: You probably believe this urban legend. Here's the truth:
entries.myth: NYC's sewers are infested with alligators that were flushed as babies and grew feeding on rats in the dark tunnels.
entries.truth: The myth started in the 1930s when a few small alligators were found in sewers. They were dumped by owners not a breeding population. Sewers are too cold for them.

entries.title: Urban Legend: The Crying Boy
entries.hook: Here's what really happened:
entries.myth: A painting of a crying boy is blamed for causing house fires. The painting is always found intact on the wall.
entries.truth: A 1985 UK newspaper claimed firefighters found the print untouched in fires. The real reason: it was mass-produced so appeared in many homes. Confirmation bias created the legend.

entries.title: Urban Legend: The Kidney Heist
entries.hook: The scariest story you've heard? It's not what you think:
entries.myth: A businessman wakes up in a bathtub of ice with a note saying 'Call 911 you've had a kidney removed.'
entries.truth: No verified case exists. Kidney transplants require tissue matching and medical infrastructure. The story spread via late-90s chain emails.

entries.title: Urban Legend: Killer in the Backseat
entries.hook: Everyone knows this urban legend. Almost none of it is true:
entries.myth: A woman driving home notices a car flashing its headlights at her. She races home and the driver tells her a man was hiding in her backseat with a knife.
entries.truth: This legend may trace to a real 1964 crime. The friendly flasher variant now appears in driver's safety courses as a real warning.

entries.title: Urban Legend: Alligators in the Sewers

entries.myth: NYC's sewers are infested with alligators that were flushed as babies and grew feeding on rats in the dark tunnels.

entries.title: Urban Legend: Bloody Mary
entries.hook: Here's what really happened:
entries.myth: Say Bloody Mary three times in front of a mirror and a ghostly woman appears to attack you. The legend has terrified children at sleepovers for decades.
entries.truth: The legend likely originated from 16th century Queen Mary I. The modern version spread in the 1970s as a harmless dare game inspired by mirror-gazing superstitions.

entries.title: Urban Legend: The Crying Boy
entries.hook: You probably believe this urban legend. Here's the truth:
entries.myth: A painting of a crying boy is blamed for causing house fires. The painting is always found intact on the wall.
entries.truth: A 1985 UK newspaper claimed firefighters found the print untouched in fires. The real reason: it was mass-produced so appeared in many homes. Confirmation bias created the legend.

entries.myth: NYC's sewers are infested with alligators that were flushed as babies and grew feeding on rats in the dark tunnels.

entries.title: Urban Legend: The Car Headlights Game

entries.title: Urban Legend: The Crying Boy
entries.hook: You've heard this story. But here's what really happened:
entries.myth: A painting of a crying boy is blamed for causing house fires. The painting is always found intact on the wall.
entries.truth: A 1985 UK newspaper claimed firefighters found the print untouched in fires. The real reason: it was mass-produced so appeared in many homes. Confirmation bias created the legend.

entries.title: Urban Legend: Alligators in the Sewers
entries.hook: The truth behind this myth is shocking:
entries.myth: NYC's sewers are infested with alligators that were flushed as babies and grew feeding on rats in the dark tunnels.
entries.truth: The myth started in the 1930s when a few small alligators were found in sewers. They were dumped by owners not a breeding population. Sewers are too cold for them.

entries.title: Urban Legend: Bloody Mary
entries.hook: Everyone knows this urban legend. Almost none of it is true:
entries.myth: Say Bloody Mary three times in front of a mirror and a ghostly woman appears to attack you. The legend has terrified children at sleepovers for decades.
entries.truth: The legend likely originated from 16th century Queen Mary I. The modern version spread in the 1970s as a harmless dare game inspired by mirror-gazing superstitions.

entries.title: Urban Legend: The Crying Boy
entries.hook: You've heard this story. But here's what really happened:
entries.myth: A painting of a crying boy is blamed for causing house fires. The painting is always found intact on the wall.
entries.truth: A 1985 UK newspaper claimed firefighters found the print untouched in fires. The real reason: it was mass-produced so appeared in many homes. Confirmation bias created the legend.

entries.title: Urban Legend: The Crying Boy
entries.hook: You probably believe this urban legend. Here's the truth:
entries.myth: A painting of a crying boy is blamed for causing house fires. The painting is always found intact on the wall.
entries.truth: A 1985 UK newspaper claimed firefighters found the print untouched in fires. The real reason: it was mass-produced so appeared in many homes. Confirmation bias created the legend.

entries.title: Urban Legend: The Kidney Heist
entries.hook: Think you know this story? Think again:
entries.myth: A businessman wakes up in a bathtub of ice with a note saying 'Call 911 you've had a kidney removed.'
entries.truth: No verified case exists. Kidney transplants require tissue matching and medical infrastructure. The story spread via late-90s chain emails.

entries.title: Urban Legend: Alligators in the Sewers
entries.hook: The scariest story you've heard? It's not what you think:
entries.myth: NYC's sewers are infested with alligators that were flushed as babies and grew feeding on rats in the dark tunnels.
entries.truth: The myth started in the 1930s when a few small alligators were found in sewers. They were dumped by owners not a breeding population. Sewers are too cold for them.

entries.hook: The scariest story you've heard? It's not what you think:
entries.myth: A taxi picks up a passenger who gets out at a cemetery. The driver looks back and the passenger has vanished. The person died exactly one year ago.
entries.truth: This is a global folklore motif dating back centuries with versions in Japanese Mexican and European cultures.

entries.title: Urban Legend: Microwaved Pet
entries.hook: The truth behind this myth is shocking:
entries.myth: An elderly woman tries to dry her wet poodle by putting it in the microwave. The pet explodes.
entries.truth: This story appeared in a 1970s book but was entirely fabricated. No documented case has ever been found.

entries.title: Urban Legend: The Car Headlights Game
entries.hook: You won't believe this story is fake:
entries.myth: Teens drive to a remote road at night stop and turn off the car. Count to three and turn headlights back on to see a ghost.
entries.truth: This is a modern campfire story that spread through social media. No paranormal sightings verified but teens still try it.
